title: Circular Motion
learningObjectives: Define angular velocity and centripetal acceleration
Apply the centripetal force formula F = mv²/r
Solve problems involving objects moving in circular paths
introText: Circular motion occurs when an object moves in a circular path at constant speed. Even though the speed is constant, the velocity changes direction, requiring a centripetal force directed toward the centre.

steps.heading: Centripetal Acceleration
steps.body:
For an object moving in a circle of radius r at speed v:

Centripetal acceleration: a_c = v²/r

This acceleration always points toward the centre of the circle. It changes the direction of velocity without changing its magnitude.
steps.deepDive: The word 'centripetal' means 'centre-seeking'. Do not confuse centripetal force with 'centrifugal force' – centrifugal force is a fictitious force in a rotating reference frame.

steps.heading: Angular Velocity and Period
steps.body:
Angular velocity (ω) measures how fast the angle changes:
ω = 2π/T = 2πf

Relationship to linear speed:
v = ωr

Centripetal force:
F_c = mv²/r = mω²r

This force must be provided by some real force: tension in a string, friction, gravity, or a normal force.
steps.deepDive: For a satellite orbiting Earth, gravity provides the centripetal force. Setting mg = mv²/r and solving gives the orbital speed.

steps.heading: Circular Motion Simulator
steps.instructionText: Adjust radius, mass and speed. Observe how angular velocity, period, and centripetal force change. The red arrow shows the velocity direction.

steps.questionText: A 0.5 kg object moves in a circle of radius 2 m at a speed of 4 m/s. What centripetal force is required?

questions.questionText: An object completes 5 full revolutions per second in a circle of radius 0.2 m. What is its angular velocity?
